title: Cardiac Diagnostics I — Final Exam (50 Questions)

questions.text: When interpreting a rhythm strip, which sequence of steps is correct?

answers.text: P waves → PR interval → QRS → rate → rhythm
Rate → rhythm → P waves → PR interval → QRS complex
QRS → P waves → PR interval → rate → rhythm
Rhythm → rate → QRS → PR interval → P waves

questions.text: A pacing site that fires at 50 bpm is most consistent with which intrinsic pacemaker?
A QRS complex is considered WIDE when it measures:
Which of the following PR-interval durations would be considered NORMAL?
On standard ECG paper, one small (1 mm) horizontal box represents how much time?
On a normal ECG, which event is recorded by the P wave?

answers.text: Conduction through the bundle of His

questions.text: Ventricular depolarization is recorded as which part of the ECG?
The T wave on a normal ECG corresponds to:
Which segment represents the early phase of ventricular repolarization?
The point at which the QRS complex transitions into the ST segment is called the:
Tall, sharply peaked T waves on an ECG are most strongly associated with which electrolyte abnormality?
Depression of the ST segment on an ECG is most commonly associated with:
ST-segment elevation on an ECG most strongly suggests:
The V1 chest electrode is positioned at the:

answers.text: 4th intercostal space, just to the right of the sternum
4th intercostal space, just to the left of the sternum

questions.text: The V6 chest electrode is placed at the:

answers.text: 4th intercostal space, right of the sternum

questions.text: How many electrodes are placed on the patient's body to acquire a standard 12-lead ECG?
Which limb electrode functions as the ground?
Which group below contains the BIPOLAR leads?
Which set of leads provides a view of the SEPTAL wall of the heart?
The INFERIOR wall of the left ventricle is best viewed by which leads?
Which combination of leads views the LATERAL wall of the left ventricle?
An inferior wall MI is most likely caused by occlusion of which artery?
Lateral wall MI is most often associated with a blockage in which artery?
ECG changes consistent with a septal or anterior wall MI suggest blockage in which artery?
A regular rhythm at 52 bpm with upright P waves before each QRS, a PR of 0.16 s, and a QRS of 0.08 s is best described as:
A regular rhythm at 130 bpm with upright sinus P waves, a PR of 0.14 s, and a narrow QRS that began gradually during exercise is consistent with:
A rhythm with normal sinus P waves and a constant PR of 0.16 s but R-R variation of more than 0.16 seconds is best described as:
Saw-toothed flutter waves at an atrial rate near 300 bpm with a regular ventricular rhythm best describe:
An irregularly irregular ventricular rhythm with a wavy fibrillatory baseline and no identifiable P waves is most consistent with:
A regular tachycardia at 200 bpm with a narrow QRS, P waves often hidden in the preceding T wave, and a PR difficult to measure is most consistent with:
A 12-lead ECG shows a slurred upstroke at the start of the QRS complex (delta wave) with a short PR interval. The most likely diagnosis is:
A premature beat that interrupts the underlying sinus rhythm with an upright P wave that is shaped DIFFERENTLY from sinus P waves and a usually narrow QRS describes:
If a P wave is visible during a junctional rhythm, it is most often:

answers.text: Tall and upright in lead II
Inverted in leads II, III, and aVF

questions.text: A junctional escape rhythm typically falls within which rate range?
A regular rhythm with inverted P waves in lead II, a PR of 0.10 s, and a narrow QRS at 140 bpm is most consistent with:
A premature, wide-and-bizarre QRS without a preceding P wave that interrupts an underlying sinus rhythm is best described as a:
Three or more ventricular escape beats in a row at a rate of 30 bpm is best described as:
Ventricular tachycardia is best defined by which set of criteria?

answers.text: ≥ 3 consecutive PVCs at a rate > 100 bpm
Wide QRS with retrograde P waves at any rate

questions.text: A polymorphic ventricular tachycardia that occurs in the setting of a long QT interval and shows a twisting morphology around the baseline is called:
A chaotic ECG pattern with no discernible rate, rhythm, P waves, PR interval, or QRS — and no pulse — best describes:
An ECG that shows essentially a flat line with no QRS complexes, no P waves, and no measurable rate is consistent with:
A regular rhythm at 75 bpm with a P wave before every QRS but a PR interval of 0.28 s (constant) is most consistent with:
A rhythm in which the PR interval lengthens with each beat until a QRS is dropped, and then the cycle repeats, is:

answers.text: Second-degree AV block, Type II (Mobitz II)

questions.text: An ECG shows P waves marching out at one regular rate and QRS complexes marching out at a different, slower regular rate, with no consistent relationship between them. The diagnosis is:
An rSR' ("rabbit ears") pattern in lead V1 with a wide QRS is most consistent with:
A small, sharp vertical line preceding each QRS complex on the rhythm strip indicates:

answers.text: Pacing spikes from a temporary pacemaker

questions.text: What is the optimal duration of continuous progressive exercise during a Graded Exercise Test (GXT)?
The MOST frequent indication for performing an exercise stress test is:

answers.text: Establishing the diagnosis of coronary artery disease (CAD)

questions.text: The Bruce protocol uses 3-minute stages and is most commonly utilized by which group?

answers.text: Patients with compensated congestive heart failure
Patients within 24 hours of a myocardial infarction

questions.text: A Holter monitor records continuously for what time period?
